name: Clearly Canadian Maple Bar Launch
description: Clearly Canadian Maple launches at 50 craft cocktail bars in Toronto. How do bartenders and premium consumers respond to it as a mixer, and what drives menu adoption and reorder?
context: Clearly Canadian is seeding Clearly Canadian Maple — a naturally maple-flavoured sparkling water — across 50 craft cocktail venues in Toronto. Bartenders receive free samples and cocktail recipe cards. The product is positioned as a premium mixer for bourbon, whiskey, and gin, and as a sober-curious mocktail base. This is an on-premise-first go-to-market strategy before retail rollout.
question_templates: How would you use Clearly Canadian Maple in a cocktail or mocktail menu?
Would you put a Clearly Canadian Maple drink on your menu permanently?
What about the maple flavour works — or doesn't work — for mixology?
Would you recommend Clearly Canadian Maple to a customer looking for a non-alcoholic option?

name: Clearly Canadian Maple Sampling at Osheaga
description: Clearly Canadian runs a Maple sampling booth at Osheaga music festival in Montreal, offering free cans and a mocktail bar. How do festival-goers respond to first taste?
context: It's a hot Saturday at Osheaga. A Clearly Canadian branded booth offers free Clearly Canadian Maple SleekCans and a mocktail bar with two Clearly Canadian Maple recipes. Attendees range from Gen Z students to Millennial music fans. Many have never heard of Clearly Canadian Maple or Clearly Canadian. The booth has no purchase option — just sample, talk, and scan a QR code.
question_templates: What's your first reaction to the maple flavour in a sparkling water?
Would you buy this at a store after trying it here?
Does a mocktail option matter to you at a festival?
Would you follow Clearly Canadian after today based on this experience?

name: Zero Sugar Shelf Discovery
description: A consumer encounters Clearly Canadian Zero Sugar for the first time on a grocery store shelf. What drives pick-up, trust, and trial for a stevia-sweetened extension of a heritage brand?
context: A health-conscious shopper pauses in the sparkling water aisle. They spot Clearly Canadian Zero Sugar — stevia-sweetened, zero grams of sugar — next to the original glass bottle version. They've seen the original before but didn't know a Zero Sugar line existed. The Zero Sugar is US-distributed only and launched in 2024 with limited marketing support.
question_templates: Does a Zero Sugar version of Clearly Canadian appeal to you?
Do you trust stevia as a sweetener — or does it put you off?
How does this compare to other zero-sugar sparkling waters you use?
Would you buy Zero Sugar over the original, or alongside it?

name: Costco In-Store Demo
description: A consumer tries Clearly Canadian at a Costco demo station during a weekend shopping trip. What triggers a bulk purchase decision and who converts on the spot?
context: It's Saturday afternoon at a Costco warehouse. A demo station offers small cups of Clearly Canadian Originals — Mountain Blackberry and Wild Cherry. A 12-pack is available on the adjacent pallet for $19.99. The demo associate explains that Clearly Canadian has been around since 1987, relaunched after a fan crowdfunding campaign, and has no artificial colours or preservatives.
question_templates: Does the Costco context make you more or less likely to take this seriously as a brand?
After tasting it, would you put the 12-pack in your cart today?
Does hearing the brand story change your perception of the product?
What would stop you from buying a 12-pack even if you liked the taste?

name: TikTok Crowdfunding Comeback Story
description: A consumer watches a TikTok video telling the story of Clearly Canadian's 2015 crowdfunding revival — 14,000 fans, 35 countries, 40,000 cases pre-ordered. How does the origin story drive brand affinity and purchase intent?
context: A 60-second TikTok tells the story: Clearly Canadian was a 90s icon that disappeared. A group of fans launched a crowdfunding campaign. 14,000 people from 35 countries pre-ordered 40,000 cases, bringing the brand back from the dead. The video has 2.3M views. The viewer has never tried Clearly Canadian but the story makes them curious.
question_templates: Does a brand comeback story like this make you want to try it?
What part of this story resonates with you most?
Would you seek out Clearly Canadian in a store after seeing this?
Does knowing it's Canadian-owned and fan-revived change how you feel about it?

name: Unexpected Nostalgia Shelf Moment
description: A consumer unexpectedly spots Clearly Canadian on a store shelf and experiences a strong nostalgia reaction. How does emotional memory drive purchase intent for lapsed and loyal consumers?
context: A shopper in their late 30s or 50s is doing a routine grocery run. They turn down the sparkling water aisle and see the Clearly Canadian embossed glass bottle — the same curved shape they remember from childhood or their teens. They haven't seen it in years and assumed it was gone. The emotional reaction is immediate.
question_templates: What memory comes back when you see this bottle?
Do you grab it on the spot or talk yourself out of it?
Has the brand aged well, or does it feel dated?
Would you buy it for nostalgia alone, or does it have to taste as good as you remember?

name: Price Comparison in the Sparkling Water Aisle
description: A consumer stands in the sparkling water aisle comparing Clearly Canadian SleekCans ($7.99/6pk) to LaCroix ($5.49/12pk) and Bubly ($4.99/12pk). What is the price-value calculus and who walks away with Clearly Canadian?
context: The shopper has a $10 budget and wants sparkling water for the week. On the shelf: Clearly Canadian SleekCans at $7.99 for 6 cans, LaCroix at $5.49 for 12 cans, Bubly at $4.99 for 12 cans. Clearly Canadian is more expensive per can but they've seen it on TikTok. LaCroix is the default. Bubly is cheaper.
question_templates: Walk me through how you make this call.
Is Clearly Canadian's price difference worth it to you?
What would Clearly Canadian need to offer to justify the premium over LaCroix?
Do you think $7.99 for 6 is expensive, fair, or cheap for a premium sparkling water?

name: "Never Plastic. Clearly Canadian." Social Post
description: A consumer sees Clearly Canadian's sustainability messaging — glass and aluminum only, no plastic — on social media. How does the no-plastic commitment shift brand perception and willingness to pay?
context: A consumer scrolling Instagram sees a Clearly Canadian post: 'Never Plastic. Clearly Canadian. Every bottle is glass. Every can is aluminum. Zero plastic, ever.' The post shows the glass bottle next to a crushed single-use plastic bottle. The comment section is full of positive responses. The shopper cares about sustainability but usually buys LaCroix in bulk plastic.
question_templates: Does a no-plastic commitment make you reconsider your sparkling water brand?
Is sustainability messaging from a beverage brand credible to you, or does it feel performative?
Would you pay more for Clearly Canadian specifically because it uses glass and aluminum?
Does this change how you feel about your current sparkling water habit?

name: 7-Eleven SleekCan Impulse Grab
description: A consumer at a 7-Eleven or Circle K checkout spots a Clearly Canadian SleekCan in the cooler. What triggers an impulse grab among convenience-channel shoppers who weren't looking for Clearly Canadian?
context: It's midday. A consumer stops at a 7-Eleven to grab a drink. They weren't planning to buy sparkling water. In the cooler: Bubly at $1.99, Perrier at $2.49, and a Clearly Canadian SleekCan (Mountain Blackberry) at $2.29. The Clearly Canadian can has a clean design and they vaguely recognize the brand. They have 30 seconds to decide.
question_templates: Do you grab the Clearly Canadian can — and what tips you toward it?
Does the brand recognition matter at all in this setting?
Is $2.29 the right price to make this a reflex buy?
Would you remember to look for it again, or is this a one-time impulse?

name: Friend Passes You a Clearly Canadian
description: A friend hands the consumer a Clearly Canadian at a social gathering and says 'try this.' How does peer recommendation shape first impressions and purchase intent for unconverted consumers?
context: At a backyard gathering, a friend pulls a Clearly Canadian glass bottle from a cooler, hands it over, and says: 'You have to try this — it's the best sparkling water.' The consumer has seen the brand before but never tried it. They crack it open. The conversation around them touches on the 90s comeback story, the glass bottle, and how it tastes different from LaCroix.
question_templates: What does it feel like to be handed this versus picking it off a shelf yourself?
Does the social context — a friend vouching for it — change how you evaluate it?
After trying it here, what's the likelihood you buy it on your next grocery run?
What would you say about it if someone asked you later?

name: Clearly Canadian Maple on the Bar Menu
description: A consumer at a bar sees a cocktail or mocktail made with Clearly Canadian Maple listed on the menu. How does on-premise visibility shape product discovery and trial intent for the new maple line?
context: A consumer is at a craft cocktail bar in Toronto. The menu features a drink called 'Maple Smoke' — Clearly Canadian Maple, bourbon, bitters, smoked salt rim — and a mocktail version called 'Maple Mule' — Clearly Canadian Maple, ginger, lime. The consumer hasn't heard of Clearly Canadian Maple before seeing it on the menu. The bartender mentions it just launched.
question_templates: Does seeing Clearly Canadian Maple on a cocktail menu make you curious to try it straight?
Would you order the mocktail version, and why?
Does a bar endorsement — being on a craft cocktail menu — change your perception of the product?
Would you look for Clearly Canadian Maple to buy for home after trying it here?

name: Weekly Sparkling Water Stock-Up Decision
description: A consumer is deciding what sparkling water to buy in bulk for the household this week. How does Clearly Canadian compete against default bulk-buy brands like LaCroix and Bubly for routine household purchases?
context: A shopper is filling their cart with the week's groceries. Their household goes through 3–4 sparkling waters a day. They usually buy LaCroix 12-packs or Bubly variety packs. They see Clearly Canadian SleekCans in a 6-pack at $7.99. They also see the glass bottles at $2.99 each. The question is whether Clearly Canadian fits into a practical weekly routine.
question_templates: Does Clearly Canadian make sense as a weekly household staple, or is it more of an occasional treat?
How does the SleekCan 6-pack at $7.99 compare to your usual bulk sparkling water spend?
Would your household finish a 6-pack of one flavour, or do you need variety?
What would it take for Clearly Canadian to replace your current default sparkling water?

name: Choosing a Beverage for a Dinner Party
description: A consumer is selecting beverages to serve at or bring to a dinner party. Does Clearly Canadian's glass bottle presentation and Canadian provenance make it a go-to for social occasions?
context: A consumer is hosting a dinner party for 8 people — a mix of wine drinkers and non-drinkers. They want a sparkling water that feels considered, not like an afterthought. They're comparing Clearly Canadian glass bottles, San Pellegrino, and Perrier. Clearly Canadian is $2.99/bottle, San Pellegrino is $2.79, Perrier is $2.49.
question_templates: Would you put Clearly Canadian on your dinner table — and does the bottle feel appropriate for the occasion?
Does Canadian provenance matter to you when you're hosting?
How does it compare to San Pellegrino or Perrier as a hosting choice?
Would the story behind the brand be something you'd mention to guests?

name: Instagram Glass Bottle Lifestyle Photo
description: A consumer sees Clearly Canadian's glass bottle in an aesthetically curated lifestyle photo on Instagram — poolside, rooftop, picnic. How does visual social discovery translate to brand curiosity and purchase intent?
context: A consumer is scrolling Instagram and pauses on a photo: a hand holding a Clearly Canadian glass bottle against a golden-hour rooftop backdrop. The post is from a lifestyle account they follow, tagged with #ClearlyCanadian. The caption reads: 'Finally found the sparkling water I've been looking for.' 14K likes. Comments full of 'where do I get this?'
question_templates: Does this kind of aesthetic post make you want to try the product?
What does the glass bottle communicate in this context versus a can?
Would you search for Clearly Canadian after seeing this post?
Is the Instagram discovery enough to drive a store visit, or do you need more information first?

name: Clearly Canadian Maple Cocktail Recipe on TikTok or Instagram
description: A consumer sees a Clearly Canadian Maple cocktail or mocktail recipe video on TikTok or Instagram. How does recipe content drive product discovery and purchase intent for the new maple line?
context: A 30-second TikTok shows a bartender making a 'Maple Sunrise Mocktail' with Clearly Canadian Maple, fresh ginger, lime, and a rosemary sprig — no alcohol. It's from a zero-proof cocktail account with 400K followers. The creator says: 'This is going to be your new fall obsession. Clearly Canadian Maple sparkling water just unlocked something.' The video has 800K views and the comment section is asking where to buy it.
question_templates: Does a recipe video make you more interested in Clearly Canadian Maple than a straight product ad would?
Would you make this mocktail at home — and would you buy Clearly Canadian Maple specifically for it?
Does the sober-curious framing resonate with you?
What would the video need to show for you to click the link and find out where to buy?

name: Shania Twain Campaign Reaction
description: Consumer sees CC's new Shania Twain × Maximum Effort ad — first campaign in 34 years. Treasure hunt through Canadian wilderness, 'Still the One' tagline, catchy jingle.
context: It's June 2025. A consumer sees Clearly Canadian's first advertisement in 34 years — a collaboration with Shania Twain and Maximum Effort Productions (Ryan Reynolds' agency). The ad follows a treasure-hunt narrative through the Canadian wilderness, with Shania arriving at a pristine BC spring. The tagline is 'Still the One.' It's cinematic, funny, and unmistakably Canadian. The ad is airing on YouTube, Instagram, and during Blue Jays broadcasts. The consumer's reaction depends on whether they know the brand, love Shania, or are encountering both for the first time.
question_templates: What's your immediate reaction to seeing Shania Twain in a sparkling water ad?
Does the 'first ad in 34 years' angle make you more curious about the brand?
Does this ad make Clearly Canadian feel nostalgic, modern, or both?
Would you seek out Clearly Canadian at a store after watching this?
Does the Ryan Reynolds / Maximum Effort connection change how you perceive the ad?

name: University Orientation Sampling
description: CC sampling booth during university orientation week. Free cans, flavour quiz on iPad, campus ambassador program.
context: It's September orientation week at a major Canadian or American university. Clearly Canadian has set up a branded sampling booth in the student commons. Campus ambassadors are handing out free SleekCans (Mountain Blackberry, Wild Cherry, Zero Sugar) and running a flavour personality quiz on an iPad. There's a selfie wall with a giant CC bottle cutout. Students can sign up as campus ambassadors for free product and merch. Most students have never tried Clearly Canadian; some have seen it on TikTok. The booth is competing for attention alongside dozens of other brands and clubs recruiting.
question_templates: Would you stop at this booth — and what would catch your eye first?
After trying a free can, would you buy one later this week on campus?
Does the flavour quiz and selfie wall make you more or less likely to engage?
Would you sign up as a campus ambassador — and what would motivate you?
Would you post about this on your social media? What would you say?

name: Blue Jays Game Discovery
description: Consumer at Blue Jays game at Rogers Centre encounters CC SleekCans. Opening day March 27, CC signage visible, three flavours available.
context: It's March 27, 2025 — Toronto Blue Jays opening day at Rogers Centre. Clearly Canadian is the team's official sparkling water. CC signage is visible behind home plate, on the scoreboard, and at concession stands. Three SleekCan flavours are available: Mountain Blackberry, Wild Cherry, and Sparkling Mineral Water, priced at $5.50 each. The consumer is a baseball fan who may or may not know Clearly Canadian. They see other fans carrying the distinctive cans. The PA announcer mentions 'Clearly Canadian — the Official Sparkling Water of the Toronto Blue Jays' between innings.
question_templates: Does seeing CC as the Blue Jays' official sparkling water change your perception of the brand?
Would you buy a CC SleekCan at the game — even at stadium pricing?
Does the Canadian identity connection (Canadian brand + Canadian team) resonate with you?
Would you look for CC at a grocery store after encountering it at the game?
Does the sports sponsorship make CC feel more mainstream and trustworthy?

name: Holiday Hostess Gift Decision
description: Consumer choosing premium beverage to bring as hostess gift for Thanksgiving or holiday dinner. Considering CC glass bottles vs wine vs other options.
context: It's late November. A consumer is choosing what to bring to a Thanksgiving or holiday dinner party. They want something more thoughtful than a bottle of wine — not everyone drinks alcohol. They're in the premium beverage aisle considering options: a 4-pack of Clearly Canadian glass bottles ($11.96), a nice bottle of wine ($15–20), a specialty sparkling cider ($8), or a box of fancy chocolates ($12). The CC glass bottles are visually striking — the curved shape and fruit labels look like a curated gift. They remember CC from childhood or have heard the comeback story.
question_templates: Would you bring Clearly Canadian glass bottles as a hostess gift — and how would the host react?
Does the glass bottle presentation feel gift-worthy compared to wine or chocolates?
Is the nostalgia/comeback story part of what makes it a good gift, or is it just about presentation?
Would you buy a CC gift pack if one existed — and what would you want in it?
Does bringing a Canadian sparkling water feel like a statement at an American dinner party?

name: Buy-Canadian Patriotic Shopping
description: Consumer actively seeking Canadian products amid US-Canada trade tensions and tariff discussions. Scanning shelves for Canadian brands as statement of national identity.
context: It's early 2025. The consumer is doing their weekly grocery shop in Canada. U.S.–Canada trade tensions are in the news — tariff threats, retaliatory measures, and social media campaigns urging Canadians to buy domestic. The consumer is actively scanning shelves for Canadian-made alternatives to American products. They pass the sparkling water aisle: LaCroix (American), Bubly (PepsiCo, American), Perrier (Nestlé, French), and Clearly Canadian — with 'Product of Canada, bottled in Canada' prominently displayed. The Hudson's Bay Company bankruptcy is fresh in the news, heightening awareness of supporting Canadian brands. The consumer feels a genuine pull to put their money behind Canadian products.
question_templates: Does the buy-Canadian sentiment make you actively seek out Clearly Canadian?
Would you switch from LaCroix or Bubly to Clearly Canadian specifically because it's Canadian?
How important is 'Product of Canada' labeling to you right now, compared to a year ago?
Does the trade tension context make CC's Canadian identity feel more meaningful or more like marketing?
Would you tell friends or family to switch to CC as part of buying Canadian?
